topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mahogany door clicked shut with a sound that echoed the finality of my resentment. I stood in the sterile, hushed office, my knuckles white where I gripped my worn messenger bag. Across the polished desk sat Mark. He looked… composed. The familiar sneer that used to bloom on his face whenever I entered a room was absent, replaced by an unnerving calm. "So," he began, his voice smooth, almost melodic, "tell me about the pressure you felt during those middle school years." I bristled, my jaw tightening. I wanted to lash out, to remind him exactly what kind of mess he had been. Instead, I forced my gaze down to my hands, tracing the frayed edges of my sleeve. "It was nothing," I managed, the lie tasting like ash. I felt a distinct heat rising in my chest, a stubborn refusal to let him see the tremor beneath my composure.

[story 2]

I watched him from the doorway, leaning against the frame, arms crossed. Mark was talking about transference, about unmet needs, and the words felt like sandpaper against my nerves. Every anecdote he shared about his past—the awkwardness, the loneliness—felt like a deliberate dismantling of my own history. A sharp, almost painful tightening occurred just beneath my ribs. I shifted my weight, deliberately letting my boots scrape lightly on the marble floor. I focused on the way the afternoon light caught the dust motes dancing in the air, anything but the unsettling intimacy of the conversation. When he finally paused, waiting for my response, I let out a short, brittle laugh that sounded more like a cough. "That’s… interesting," I said, the tone carefully neutral, though my eyes betrayed a deep-seated urge to push him away, to make him uncomfortable again.

[story 3]

My breath hitched when he looked up, those familiar, assessing eyes meeting mine. It was a collision of old antagonism and something entirely new—a strange, fragile vulnerability radiating from him. I felt a sudden, intense need to prove that the old dynamic still held sway, that I was still the one in control of the narrative. I crossed my arms tightly over my chest, a defensive posture honed over years of navigating his presence. "You think you know everything about me now?" I challenged, my voice pitched low, laced with a deliberate edge of challenge. I wanted him to flinch, to recall the times I had been the one holding the power. Instead, he simply offered a small, knowing smile, a gesture that infuriated me with its easy acceptance. I swallowed hard, fighting the urge to stomp my foot, focusing instead on the faint, metallic tang of adrenaline flooding my system.
